Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Early Modern

Margaret Cavendish

1623 – 1673

Self-moving matter and a fully embodied philosophy of mind.

Margaret Cavendish’s philosophy of mind is best reconstructed as a theory of corporeal, self-moving, distributed, and graded mentality. Her mature natural philosophy rejects the Cartesian picture of a non-bodily human mind lodged in, or mysteriously joined to, an otherwise mindless body. The human mind is not a simple immaterial ego. It is a material, rational, self-moving part of Nature, inseparably mixed with sensitive and inanimate matter throughout the organism. At the same time, Cavendish does not simply “secularize away” God, the divine soul, angels, or spirits. She repeatedly marks a boundary between natural philosophy, which concerns corporeal Nature, and theology or faith, which concerns God and supernatural beings. That boundary is not a dismissal; it is a central feature of her system. It tells us what kind of mind can be philosophically explained by natural reason and what kind of spiritual reality must be treated as divine, supernatural, or epistemically beyond human natural cognition.

A useful first formula is this: the natural soul is material; the divine soul, God, and sometimes angels or spirits belong to a different order of discourse. Cavendish’s natural theory of mind is therefore materialist, but it is not reductively mechanist. Matter is not inert stuff pushed around from outside. Nature is an infinite corporeal body with self-moving, self-knowing parts. This gives Cavendish a theory that is at once anti-Cartesian, anti-mechanist, anti-Scholastic, vitalist, and in a carefully qualified sense panpsychist or panorganic.

1. Corpus and chronological caution

Cavendish’s views develop across several works. The most important texts for this topic are Philosophical and Physical Opinions; Philosophical Letters of 1664; Observations upon Experimental Philosophy of 1666, often read together with The Blazing World; and Grounds of Natural Philosophy of 1668, which is explicitly presented as a second edition “much altered” from Philosophical and Physical Opinions.

This chronology matters because Cavendish’s language about “spirits,” “souls,” angels, and finite immaterial beings is not perfectly stable. In Philosophical Letters, she clarifies that when she previously used language of “spirits,” she meant material, not immaterial, spirits; she also says that her earlier writings were immature and had been corrected or altered. In Grounds, she moves even more sharply toward the view that only God is truly immaterial, while created spirits and spiritual souls must be something other than immaterial beings in the strict sense.

This report therefore treats Cavendish’s philosophy of mind as a structured but developing system. The natural mind is consistently material and self-moving. The harder interpretive questions concern the divine soul, angels, devils, and finite spirits, where Cavendish sometimes preserves orthodox theological possibilities and sometimes presses her materialism further.

2. The metaphysical foundation: Nature is corporeal, self-moving, and self-knowing

Cavendish’s philosophy of mind begins from her metaphysics of Nature. Nature is not a collection of passive particles externally moved by impact alone. Nature is one infinite material body, internally differentiated into parts, and those parts possess self-motion and self-knowledge. In Observations, she says that matter, self-motion, and self-knowledge are inseparable, and that Nature is a material, self-moving, self-knowing body. She also insists that nothing is part of Nature unless it is corporeal; spiritual beings, if there are any, are not parts of Nature in the same way.

The mature ontology is built around three inseparable “degrees” or kinds of matter: rational matter, sensitive matter, and inanimate matter. Rational and sensitive matter are animate and self-moving; inanimate matter is not self-moving in itself, but it is always intermixed with the animate degrees. Rational matter is more subtle, free, and designing; sensitive matter is more laboring, executive, or architectonical; inanimate matter is the grosser bodily material that is carried, ordered, shaped, or worked upon by the animate degrees. Cavendish repeatedly denies that these degrees exist as isolated substances: they are “so joined” that every natural creature and every natural part is a composed unity of all three.

This three-degree theory is the key to Cavendish’s philosophy of mind. “Mind” and “soul” are not detachable entities superadded to body. They are functions or organizations of rational and sensitive matter within a corporeal whole. In an early formulation, she describes rational animate matter as the “Infinite Mind or Soul” of infinite matter, sensitive animate matter as its life, and inanimate matter as body. In later works, the same architecture remains, but the terminology becomes more careful: the natural soul of a human being is the rational, corporeal, self-moving part of Nature within that human being.

Cavendish’s anti-dualism rests partly on a strict corporealism about natural motion and perception. She denies that there can be natural motion without matter, or natural perception without a corporeal perceiver. A “substance” that is not body is, for natural philosophy, nearly inconceivable: there is no middle thing between body and no body. Even extremely subtle “spiritual” substance, if it is created and natural, would still have the properties of body, such as divisibility and composability.

3. The natural soul

Cavendish’s most important distinction is between the natural soul and the divine or supernatural soul. The natural soul is not the immortal theological soul considered by the Church. It is the rational, self-moving, corporeal part of the creature. In Observations, she explicitly says that the natural soul of man is best viewed as a material self-moving substance, a part of Nature’s soul; she then says she leaves the divine soul of man to the Church.

This is not a minor aside. It means Cavendish has a full naturalistic theory of cognition, perception, imagination, passion, appetite, bodily coordination, and agency, while refusing to claim that natural philosophy can explain the supernatural destiny of the divine soul. The natural mind is the subject matter of philosophy and natural reason; the divine soul is a subject matter of faith, revelation, and ecclesial doctrine.

The natural soul is also not exclusively human. Cavendish denies that human beings alone possess sense, reason, or natural soul. Because every natural creature is composed of rational, sensitive, and inanimate matter, every natural creature has some kind of natural sense and reason. A stone, for example, does not have animal sensation or human rationality, but it still has the rational soul of Nature according to its mineral kind. The difference between human, animal, vegetable, mineral, and elemental cognition is not the difference between minded and mindless being; it is a difference of mode, figure, organization, proportion, and kind-specific motion.

This is why Cavendish’s doctrine often sounds panpsychist, but it is not a flat claim that all things think as humans think. It is rather a theory of graded, kind-specific, corporeal mentality. The rational and sensitive degrees of matter are present throughout Nature, but they do not everywhere produce human deliberation, discursive thought, self-report, or animal pain. Cavendish’s “soul” is a natural principle of self-moving, perceptive, knowing organization.

4. The human mind: rational matter distributed through the body

For Cavendish, the human mind is not the brain alone, nor an immaterial substance attached to the brain. It is the rational matter of the human creature. This rational matter is mixed throughout the body with sensitive and inanimate matter. Cavendish therefore gives a strongly embodied and distributed account of cognition. The head and brain may have special functions, but they do not monopolize knowledge. The heart, liver, stomach, hand, eye, ear, and other parts have their own sensitive and rational motions.

This point is central to her anti-Cartesianism. Descartes separates thinking substance from extended substance. Cavendish refuses that division. A mind that moves, perceives, thinks, divides, composes, conflicts, and cooperates must itself be material, because motion, division, and composition are corporeal features. The Stanford Encyclopedia summarizes her core doctrine as the claim that all things in the universe, including human minds, are material, while also noting her careful theological qualifications concerning divine or supernatural soul.

Recent scholarship has sharpened this point by emphasizing Cavendish’s “materiality of mind” argument from mental conflict. Colin Chamberlain notes that Cavendish treats the mind as literally composite: one mind can be divided against itself, loving and hating, desiring and resisting, or entertaining contrary passions. For Cavendish, such conflict is not merely metaphorical. It shows that the mind has parts, factions, motions, and internal plurality; and to have parts in this way is to be corporeal.

The human mind is therefore a mereological society of rational motions rather than a simple indivisible substance. It can be unified enough to count as one mind, yet internally plural enough to undergo conflict, distraction, passion, imagination, deliberation, and error. The unity of mind is not the unity of metaphysical simplicity; it is the unity of organized corporeal composition.

5. Mind-body relation: not interaction, but internal coordination

Cavendish does not need a Cartesian “mind-body interaction” theory, because mind and body are not two heterogeneous substances. The human creature is one corporeal being composed of rational, sensitive, and inanimate matter. The rational parts design, deliberate, imagine, and direct; the sensitive parts execute, pattern, labor, and manage bodily operations; the inanimate parts provide the gross material body.

This does not mean the rational parts mechanically command the sensitive parts. Cavendish repeatedly describes a looser relation of information, imitation, agreement, persuasion, and cooperation. The rational parts may persuade or command, but the sensitive parts are not absolutely bound to obey. When they agree, bodily action is orderly; when they disagree, disorder, disease, error, or conflict may arise. This is a surprisingly non-despotic model of embodiment: the mind is not a sovereign ghost ruling a passive machine, but one active degree of matter negotiating with other active degrees.

Her physiology of action is thus distributed. Digestion, growth, healing, pain, disease, memory-like retention, sense perception, and bodily skill cannot be explained by a centralized immaterial mind issuing commands to mindless organs. Body parts “know” their own offices because sensitive and rational matter are present throughout the organism. Cavendish explicitly denies that the head or brain can engross all knowledge.

This yields a philosophy of mind that is embodied in a very strong sense. The body is not merely the mind’s instrument; the body’s parts are themselves partially cognitive. The “mind” is not outside the body, nor merely inside one privileged organ. It is the rational organization of a living material whole.

6. Perception: self-motion occasioned by objects

Cavendish’s theory of perception is one of her most distinctive contributions. She denies that perception is caused by external objects impressing themselves upon passive sense organs. Objects do not transmit motion into perceivers in a simple impact model. Rather, the perceiving body’s own self-moving sensitive and rational matter produces a perceptive pattern on the occasion of encountering an object.

In human vision, for example, the eye’s sensitive matter patterns the visible object, while rational matter may produce a corresponding rational figure or conception in its own substance. But Cavendish also warns that not all perception is patterning in the human visual sense. Wood, stone, or metal may perceive a human being without perceiving in the way a human eye does. Each kind of creature has perceptions appropriate to its own structure and motions.

Perception is therefore not passive reception. It is self-moving, active, interpretive corporeal patterning. Cavendish’s world is full of perceptive activity, but that activity is plural and species-specific. Human beings should not assume that their form of perception is the measure of all perception.

She distinguishes sensitive perception from rational perception. Sensitive perception is associated with the organs and bodily patterning; rational perception is associated with thoughts, conceptions, imagination, and rational figures. These two perceptions often work together, but they can diverge. A person deep in contemplation may fail to notice a touch, not because the body has no sensitive perception, but because the rational motions are occupied elsewhere.

This is a powerful account of attention and dissociation. The body may perceive locally while the rational mind is directed elsewhere. One hand may not know exactly what the other perceives, but the parts of the body maintain enough commerce, acquaintance, and agreement to produce general intelligence in the organism.

7. Thought, imagination, and fancy

Cavendish treats thoughts, fancies, imaginations, and conceptions as motions of rational matter. They are not immaterial acts. Rational matter can move “in its own substance” without always depending on present sensitive perception. That is why human beings can imagine absent objects, construct fictional worlds, deliberate, speculate, dream, and philosophize.

Her concept of fancy is especially important. Fancy is not mere frivolity or irrational ornament. It is a rational power of figuration and world-making. This matters for reading The Blazing World, which Cavendish herself describes as partly philosophical and partly fanciful. The fictional form is not external to the philosophy; it is one of the ways rational matter explores possible figures, orders, worlds, and metaphysical hypotheses.

Spanish-language scholarship on Cavendish usefully emphasizes this point: fancy and reason are not simply opposed. Cavendish’s writing explores matter, living substance, mind, reason, spirit, sensation, and thought through literary as well as argumentative means. Her imagination is a rational faculty with philosophical work to do.

This is also why Cavendish’s philosophical style should not be treated as a defect in rigor. Her dialogues, letters, fictions, poems, and speculative images are continuous with her theory of rational matter. A mind that thinks by making figures may philosophize by making worlds.

8. Is Cavendish a panpsychist?

Cavendish is often classified as a panpsychist because she refuses to restrict mentality to human beings or even animals. The Stanford Encyclopedia says that she does not restrict thinking to human brains and that mentality is ubiquitous throughout the universe. The Internet Encyclopedia similarly describes her as defending a form of panpsychism in which natural bodies and body parts possess minds or mental properties.

That classification is defensible, but only with qualifications. Cavendish does not say that every thing has a human-like consciousness. She does not flatten the difference between human deliberation, animal sense, plant-like growth, mineral organization, and elemental motions. Her claim is that every natural body contains rational and sensitive matter, and therefore has some form of self-knowledge, perception, and reason according to its kind.

A more exact label might be qualified panpsychism, panorganic materialism, or vitalist materialism. “Panpsychism” captures the ubiquity of mentality; “panorganic” captures Cavendish’s tendency to treat Nature as a living, organized, self-moving body; “materialism” captures her insistence that natural minds are corporeal; and “vitalism” captures the fact that matter is active, animate, and internally ordered rather than inert. Portuguese and Spanish reception sources describe her in just these overlapping terms: as a thinker of self-moving cognitive matter, combining materialism, vitalism, and panpsychism or panorganicism.

The safest formulation is: Cavendish is a panpsychist if panpsychism means that mind-like properties are pervasive in Nature; she is not a panpsychist if that means every object has the same type of consciousness or a unified personal subjectivity.

9. God and the divine mind

God is not an optional ornament in Cavendish’s metaphysics. God marks the absolute limit of Nature and natural philosophy. In Grounds, Cavendish distinguishes God and Nature with great care: God is eternal, infinite, immaterial, immovable, immutable, indivisible, incompoundable, perfect, and inalterable; Nature is infinite and corporeal, moving, mutable, divisible, compoundable, imperfect, and alterable. God knows Nature perfectly, while Nature does not know God perfectly.

This is crucial for any reconstruction of Cavendish’s philosophy of mind. God’s knowledge is not a larger version of human rational matter. The divine “mind,” if we use that phrase, is not made of rational matter, is not moved by corporeal self-motion, is not divided into parts, and does not perceive by patterning external objects. God’s knowledge is divine, immaterial, and perfect; natural cognition is corporeal, finite, partial, and often irregular.

Cavendish also insists that no corporeal part of Nature can naturally perceive an immaterial being. A corporeal perceiver can perceive corporeal objects; an immaterial being is not a corporeal object fit for natural perception. Yet she also says that all parts of Nature have an innate notion of God and that all creatures worship God in some manner. This is a subtle position: God’s essence is naturally incomprehensible, but Nature is nonetheless oriented toward God.

This produces a distinctive theological epistemology. Natural reason cannot form an adequate idea of God’s essence, but Nature can acknowledge God’s existence, power, and superiority. Cavendish’s doctrine that all creatures have some notion of God should be read as part of her philosophy of mind: cognition is not merely instrumental problem-solving; at the level of Nature as a whole, cognition includes worship, acknowledgment, and orientation toward divine supremacy.

Lisa Sarasohn Detlefsen’s work on Cavendish and God is helpful here. Detlefsen argues that God plays a more significant role in Cavendish’s natural philosophy than one might expect if one focused only on Cavendish’s separation of theology from natural philosophy. God is not a mechanical mover inside Nature, but God is still the ultimate source of Nature’s power, order, and normative standards. Detlefsen also notes a real tension: Cavendish wants to separate natural philosophy from theology, yet God remains important for explaining order, teleology, and divine governance.

For philosophy of mind, this means that Cavendish has a two-tier theory of cognition. Natural cognition is material, self-moving, finite, perceptive, and fallible. Divine knowledge is immaterial, unmoving, perfect, and not naturally representable by human ideas. The two are not species within one genus of “mind” unless that genus is very abstract. God’s mind is not the apex of natural rational matter; it is ontologically other.

10. Angels, devils, spirits, and angelic minds

Cavendish’s treatment of angels and spirits is one of the most delicate parts of her system. It cannot be dismissed as irrelevant theology, because angels and spirits are boundary cases for the question “Can there be mind without body?”

In the mid-1660s, Cavendish often separates natural philosophy from theology by saying that natural philosophy concerns what can be known through sense, reason, and observation, while theology concerns faith. On this scheme, the natural is material and the supernatural is immaterial. Duncan’s study of Cavendish on the supernatural notes that in this period she can speak of angels, devils, and the divine human soul as supernatural beings: not parts of Nature, not natural bodies, and not objects of natural philosophical explanation.

The Blazing World dramatizes this boundary. Its spirits discuss immaterial souls and material souls, and the work contains a striking statement that a composed material body can contain numerous material natural souls because every material part has a material natural soul. Nature itself is described as one infinite self-moving, living, self-knowing body composed of inanimate, sensitive, and rational matter. Even in a fictional-spiritual setting, the theory of material natural soul remains central.

By Grounds in 1668, Cavendish pushes more strongly against finite immaterial beings. She says she cannot conceive how an immaterial can be in Nature, and that an immaterial being must be uncreated, which can only be God. Created spirits and spiritual souls, she reasons, must be something other than immaterial; otherwise they would be perilously close to “petty gods.” She also says that while Scripture names angels and devils as spirits, it does not clearly declare whether those spirits are immaterial, and she considers it probable that spirits are material, especially because Heaven and Hell are described materially.

The result is not a simple denial of angels. It is a disciplined metaphysical narrowing. Cavendish increasingly resists the idea of finite immaterial minds. God may be immaterial because God is uncreated, infinite, indivisible, and omnipotent. But a created finite being with cognition, agency, or motion seems, on Cavendishian principles, to require body, divisibility, and corporeal self-motion. If angels are immaterial, they belong to supernatural theology and cannot be naturally understood. If angels are created natural spirits, they are probably material and therefore fall under the general theory of corporeal mind.

This is philosophically important. Cavendish’s angelology tests the limits of her materialism. Her mature tendency is to deny that created minds can be strictly immaterial, while still preserving reverence toward Scripture and theological mystery.

11. The divine or supernatural human soul

The same caution applies to the human divine soul. Cavendish’s natural philosophy explains the human natural mind: rational matter, sensitive perception, passions, imagination, bodily knowledge, and distributed cognition. But she does not always claim that this exhausts what Christian theology means by the immortal or divine soul.

In Observations, she explicitly distinguishes the natural soul from the divine soul and leaves the latter to the Church. Duncan’s study notes that for several years Cavendish allowed a finite supernatural soul in a way that set her apart from a purely Hobbesian materialism, though the relation between such a supernatural soul and the material body remains mysterious. The Internet Encyclopedia likewise notes that Cavendish later qualifies or amends her earlier treatment of immaterial soul, and that by the time of Grounds she appears to move toward the view that God alone is immaterial.

The philosophical point is this: the natural soul does explanatory work; the divine soul does not function as a natural-philosophical mechanism. The natural soul explains perception, thought, action, imagination, passion, and bodily coordination. The divine soul, if affirmed, is a supernatural reality known by faith rather than a posit within natural science.

Cavendish thereby avoids two extremes. She does not reduce Christian soul-language to crude physiology. But she also refuses to let an unexplained immaterial soul do the explanatory work of a natural philosophy of mind. Her natural psychology is materialist; her theological reserve is real.

12. Freedom, agency, and mental conflict

Cavendish’s account of self-motion yields a distinctive theory of agency. Every self-moving part has a kind of freedom because its motion proceeds from itself rather than being merely imposed from outside. But this freedom is never absolute. Each part exists within larger compositions, resistances, agreements, and divine limits.

French scholarship on Cavendish has usefully emphasized that her self-motions are spontaneous or voluntary in a broad natural sense, while also noting that this does not eliminate all mechanical or constrained behavior. Cavendish’s sensitive parts may fail to consent to rational direction, which helps explain bodily disorder and the limits of conscious control.

For the philosophy of mind, this gives Cavendish a non-simple view of will. The will is not a single faculty of an indivisible soul. It is an organized but conflict-prone motion or set of motions within rational matter, embedded in a body whose sensitive parts also have their own active tendencies. Appetite, temperance, desire, aversion, attention, distraction, and passion become negotiations among corporeal parts rather than commands issued by a metaphysical monarch.

Chamberlain’s interpretation of Cavendish’s “passionate conflict” is especially relevant: mental division is evidence of material composition. A purely simple immaterial mind should not be capable of the kind of internal opposition Cavendish describes. Human minds conflict because they are made of active parts.

13. Nonhuman minds and the individuation problem

Cavendish’s universal mentality raises a hard question: if all things contain rational and sensitive matter, what individuates one mind from another? Why is there a human mind, a horse mind, a tree mind, a stone mind, or a local organ’s knowledge rather than just one cosmic mind?

Her answer is compositional and figurative. Natural parts divide, compose, join, separate, pattern, and organize into particular figures. A creature’s mind is the rational matter of that creature organized according to that creature’s figure and motions. The same three degrees of matter exist everywhere, but different creatures have different proportions, arrangements, regularities, and kinds of motion.

French reception has foregrounded this individuation problem. A French study on Cavendish notes that all individuals share the same basic nature because the same three forms or degrees of matter are intermixed everywhere; individuation therefore has to be explained by the varying organization and quantity of these degrees, not by radically different substances.

This is one of Cavendish’s most philosophically fertile ideas. Minds are not individuated by immaterial souls sealed inside bodies. They are individuated by organized corporeal figures within Nature. A mind is an active pattern of rational matter belonging to a creaturely composition.

14. Cavendish against Descartes, Hobbes, and Henry More

Against Descartes, Cavendish denies the split between res cogitans and res extensa. Thinking is not the mark of an immaterial substance; it is an activity of rational matter. Body is not mindless extension; it contains sensitive and rational matter throughout.

Against Hobbes, Cavendish denies that matter is inert and that perception can be adequately explained by external pressure, collision, and transferred motion. Her matter is self-moving and self-knowing. She is a materialist, but not a mechanist in the Hobbesian sense.

Against Henry More and other defenders of immaterial spirit, Cavendish argues that extension, divisibility, composition, motion, and passion point toward materiality. If a mind has parts, movements, passions, and internal conflict, it belongs to corporeal Nature rather than to the immaterial divine order. Chamberlain’s study shows how Cavendish’s account of passions directly contests More’s attempt to defend an extended but immaterial spirit.

This triadic contrast helps locate Cavendish’s originality. She rejects Cartesian dualism without becoming Hobbesian mechanism, and she rejects immaterialist psychology without reducing mind to dead matter. Her alternative is a living materialism of self-moving rational and sensitive matter.

15. Non-English reception: useful emphases beyond the Anglophone frame

The French materials I found tend to emphasize three things: the intermixture of the three degrees of matter, the individuation problem produced by that intermixture, and the relation between Cavendish’s vitalism and her theory of spontaneous motion. One French article stresses that Cavendish posits one matter with several degrees—rational, sensitive, and inanimate—and that these degrees are mixed in all individuals, making individuation a central problem. Another French piece on Cavendish and Conway treats the supernatural in The Blazing World as philosophically significant rather than merely decorative: immaterial spirits, if admitted, belong outside Nature and to faith, while material vehicles or material souls remain crucial for natural manifestation.

The Spanish-language sources I found emphasize Cavendish’s place among seventeenth-century women philosophers contesting the Cartesian separation of body, reason, and mind. They draw attention to the philosophical role of fancy, poetry, embodiment, and distributed bodily knowledge. One Spanish article stresses that Cavendish, Anne Conway, and Elisabeth of Bohemia all engaged questions of materiality, immateriality, reason, body, and emotion; another highlights Cavendish’s treatment of imagination as a rational faculty rather than an irrational supplement.

A Spanish reception piece describes Cavendish’s view in terms of materialism, panorganicism, and panpsychism: rationality and sensitivity are not confined to the brain, and all things have some degree of self-motion and self-knowledge. A Portuguese translation project similarly frames Philosophical Letters as challenging mechanistic and atomistic views by conceiving matter as self-moving and cognitive.

These non-English sources do not overturn the main Anglophone interpretation, but they shift the emphasis. They make Cavendish look less like a quirky precursor to analytic panpsychism and more like a thinker of embodied cognition, literary-rational imagination, spiritual boundary cases, and the metaphysics of individuation.

A reconstructed Cavendishian philosophy of mind

The following theses extract a systematic philosophy of mind from Cavendish’s texts. 1. Natural-corporeal closure. Everything that belongs to Nature is corporeal. Natural mind, natural perception, natural knowledge, and natural soul must therefore be material. 2. No natural immaterial motion. Motion cannot exist without matter. Since thinking, perceiving, imagining, desiring, and conflicting are motions or actions, natural thought must be corporeal. 3. Three-degree matter. Nature is composed everywhere of rational, sensitive, and inanimate matter. These are not separate substances but inseparable degrees of one material Nature. 4. Natural soul as rational matter. The natural soul of a creature is its rational corporeal self-motion; in human beings, this is the natural mind. The divine soul is a different theological matter. 5. Sensitive life. Sensitive matter is the active, bodily, perceptive, laboring degree that patterns objects, manages organs, executes bodily functions, and cooperates with rational matter. 6. Distributed cognition. The mind is not confined to the brain. Sense and reason exist in all parts of the body, though in different modes and with different functions. 7. The body is cognitive. Organs and body parts have their own local knowledge. Digestion, growth, disease, pain, and skilled bodily action involve sensitive and rational corporeal knowledge. 8. Perception is active self-motion. Objects occasion perception but do not mechanically transmit perception into passive organs. Perception is produced by the perceiver’s self-moving matter. 9. Double perception. Human perception usually involves both sensitive and rational perception: bodily organ-patterning and rational conception or figuration. These can cooperate or diverge. 10. Thought and imagination are rational motions. Fancies, imaginations, conceptions, and speculative worlds are motions of rational matter, not immaterial acts. 11. Mind is composite. A human mind is a structured plurality of rational motions. It can divide, conflict, and recombine. Mental conflict is evidence of material composition. 12. Agency is self-motion under constraint. Natural parts have free self-motion, but they are constrained by composition, by other parts, by Nature’s order and irregularity, and ultimately by God’s decrees. 13. No despotic mind-body command. Rational matter may direct or persuade sensitive matter, but sensitive matter has its own activity. Bodily order depends on agreement, not on a ghostly sovereign forcing inert organs. 14. Kind-specific mentality. All natural creatures have some sense and reason, but not all have human sense and reason. A stone, plant, animal, and human being differ by figure, motion, organization, and kind. 15. Qualified panpsychism. Cavendish’s Nature is pervasively minded, but this does not imply universal human-like consciousness. Her view is better described as graded panpsychism or panorganic vitalist materialism. 16. God is not a natural mind. God has perfect knowledge, but divine knowledge is immaterial, indivisible, immutable, and not a form of rational matter. God is ontologically distinct from Nature. 17. Natural ignorance of the divine essence. No corporeal part of Nature can naturally perceive or adequately conceive an immaterial being. Nature can have an innate notion of God, but not perfect knowledge of God. 18. The divine soul is not a natural explanatory posit. Cavendish’s natural soul explains cognition and bodily life; the divine soul, if affirmed, belongs to theology and faith. 19. Angelic minds are boundary cases. If angels are immaterial, they are supernatural and not naturally intelligible as corporeal minds. If they are created spirits within Nature, Cavendish increasingly suggests they must be material. 20. Theology is not disposable. God, divine soul, and spirits are not distractions; they define the limits of natural philosophy and prevent Cavendish’s materialism from becoming a totalizing denial of faith.

Main interpretive problems

The first unresolved problem is consciousness. Cavendish speaks constantly of knowledge, perception, sense, reason, self-knowledge, and intelligence, but she does not map neatly onto the modern distinction between access consciousness, phenomenal consciousness, intentionality, and cognition. Calling her a panpsychist is plausible, but only if one avoids assuming that “knowledge” always means reflective experience or human-like subjectivity.

The second problem is unity. If every part has perception and knowledge, what makes one human mind one mind? Cavendish’s answer seems to be compositional: bodily parts form a creaturely society, with general intelligence emerging from commerce and agreement among parts. But this leaves a genuine philosophical difficulty about personal unity.

The third problem is the supernatural soul. Cavendish’s natural soul is clearly material. Her divine soul is treated with reverent restraint and often left to the Church. But her position on finite immaterial souls shifts, and Grounds pushes strongly toward the view that only God is immaterial. This means a responsible reconstruction must not force all texts into one perfectly consistent doctrine.

The fourth problem is God’s relation to Nature. Cavendish wants natural philosophy to explain Nature through matter and self-motion, yet she also assigns God a role as creator, governor, source of power, object of universal worship, and ground of order. Detlefsen is right that a real tension remains here. But that tension is philosophically productive: it keeps Cavendish’s materialism from becoming atheistic naturalism while preserving her refusal to use God as a mechanical cause inside Nature.

Conclusion

Cavendish’s philosophy of mind can be reconstructed as a corporeal theory of soul. The natural soul is rational matter; perception is self-moving corporeal patterning; the human mind is distributed through the body; bodily parts have local knowledge; passions and conflicts show that mind is composite; and all of Nature is pervaded by graded forms of sense, reason, life, and self-knowledge.

But the theory is not simply secular materialism. God is the immaterial, indivisible, perfect knower whom Nature cannot fully comprehend. The divine soul is treated as a theological reality outside natural explanation. Angels and spirits are boundary cases that force Cavendish to clarify whether created minds can be immaterial; her mature answer tends toward no, unless they are treated as supernatural mysteries rather than natural beings.

The resulting philosophy of mind is one of the most ambitious alternatives to Cartesian dualism in the seventeenth century: not mind versus body, but minded body; not inert matter plus immaterial soul, but self-moving rational and sensitive matter; not a single centralized ego, but a distributed corporeal intelligence; not a denial of God, but a rigorous distinction between finite natural minds and divine incomprehensible knowledge.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
